content:
# Deuteronomy 19 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 19 (21 verses) | **Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents compared:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

## v.1 — "dwell" vs. "settle"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "dwell in their cities and in their houses" |
| LOGOS | "settle in their cities and in their houses" |
| PNEUMA | "settle in their towns and houses" |

**Ruling:** "Settle" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). More idiomatic English with "cities and houses." PNEUMA also uses "towns" rather than "cities" — consensus follows EMET/LOGOS in retaining "cities" (*'arim*) as the formal term.

---

## v.3 — Reflexive "for yourself" on road clause

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "You shall prepare for yourself the way" |
| LOGOS | "You shall prepare the road for yourself" |
| PNEUMA | "Calculate the distances carefully and divide the land" (paraphrase) |

**Ruling:** Consensus follows LOGOS's rendering ("prepare the road") without the reflexive phrase, which clusters with the "for yourself" of v.2. PNEUMA's paraphrase introduces "calculate distances" without textual basis and is not adopted.

---

## v.3 — "territory" / "land" distinction

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "divide into three parts the territory of your land" |
| LOGOS | "divide into three parts the territory of your land" |
| PNEUMA | "divide the land the LORD your God is giving you" |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS retain "territory" (Hebrew *gevul*). PNEUMA collapses "territory" and "land" into one noun. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS for precision.

---

## v.4 — "and live" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "to save his life" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "who shall flee there and live" |
| LOGOS | "who shall flee there and live" |
| PNEUMA | "who flees there to save his life" |

**Ruling:** "And live" (EMET/LOGOS) is the literal *ve-chai* and is more economical. PNEUMA adds explanatory intent not in MT. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.5 — "the iron head slips" vs. "the head flies off the handle"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the iron head slips from the wood" |
| LOGOS | "the iron head slips from the wood" |
| PNEUMA | "the head flies off the handle" |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS: "slips from the wood" (*nasha'l min ha-ets*). PNEUMA: "flies off the handle" — idiomatic but introduces an interpretive rendering ("handle") not in MT. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.6 — "while his heart is hot" vs. "in a rage"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "because his heart is hot" |
| LOGOS | "while his heart is hot" |
| PNEUMA | "in a rage" |

**Ruling:** The literal idiom *ki yicham levavo* ("for/while his heart is hot") is preserved by EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA replaces it with "in a rage," which accurately conveys meaning but loses the Hebrew body-heat metaphor. Consensus follows LOGOS ("while his heart is hot").

---

## v.9 — "all the days" vs. "always"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "to walk in his ways all the days" |
| LOGOS | "to walk in his ways always" |
| PNEUMA | "to walk in his ways always" |

**Ruling:** "Always" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is idiomatic for *kol ha-yamim* in this context. Consensus adopts "always."

---

## v.10 — "blood-guilt come upon you" (EMET) vs. "bloodguilt not be upon you" (LOGOS) vs. "blood guilt will not rest on you" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "blood-guilt come upon you" |
| LOGOS | "bloodguilt not be upon you" |
| PNEUMA | "blood guilt will not rest on you" |

**Ruling:** Minor structural difference. All convey the same concept. Consensus hyphenates "blood-guilt" (EMET) and retains the positive warning form: "blood-guilt come upon you" — preserving the conditional threat of the Hebrew *ve-hayah alekha damim*.

---

## v.13 — "from Yisra'el" (EMET) vs. "from Israel" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "from Yisra'el" |
| LOGOS | "from Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "from Israel" |

**Ruling:** Project convention uses English name forms. Consensus: "from Israel."

---

## v.16 — "apostasy" vs. "wrongdoing" vs. "a crime"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "to testify against him of apostasy" |
| LOGOS | "to accuse him of wrongdoing" |
| PNEUMA | "accuses someone of a crime" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *sarah* = deviation, wrong-turning. EMET's "apostasy" is too theologically loaded for a civil witness law. PNEUMA's "a crime" is too generic. LOGOS's "wrongdoing" is the best fit for the formal legal register. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.17 — "two men who have the dispute" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "the two parties involved" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the two men who have the dispute" |
| LOGOS | "both men who have the dispute" |
| PNEUMA | "the two parties involved in the dispute" |

**Ruling:** Minor variation. Consensus follows LOGOS ("both men who have the dispute"), which is natural and precise.

---

## v.18 — "inquire carefully" vs. "inquire diligently" vs. "investigate the matter thoroughly"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the judges shall inquire carefully" |
| LOGOS | "The judges shall inquire diligently" |
| PNEUMA | "The judges shall investigate the matter thoroughly" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *darash heytev* = "inquire well/thoroughly." "Diligently" (LOGOS) and "carefully" (EMET) are close synonyms; "thoroughly" (PNEUMA) is accurate but adds "the matter" not in MT. Consensus follows LOGOS ("diligently").

---

## v.19 — "intended" vs. "schemed"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "as he intended to do to his brother" |
| LOGOS | "as he had intended to do to his brother" |
| PNEUMA | "what he schemed to do to the other person" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *zamam* = to plan, devise. "Intended" (EMET/LOGOS) fits the legal register. PNEUMA's "schemed" adds moral color and "the other person" loses the kinship term "brother" (*achiv*). Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.20 — "those who remain" vs. "the rest" vs. "the rest of the people"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "those who remain shall hear and shall fear" |
| LOGOS | "the rest shall hear and be afraid" |
| PNEUMA | "The rest of the people will hear about this and be afraid" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ha-nish'arim* = "the ones remaining." All render the deterrent function. PNEUMA adds "about this" and "the people" without textual basis. Consensus follows LOGOS ("the rest shall hear and fear") — slightly more literal than LOGOS's "be afraid" but "fear" preserves the Hebrew *yir'u*.

---

*SOPHIA — Deuteronomy 19 disagreements logged: 13*
